one_line_takeaway: Both theory (a robust-generalization bound scaling as sqrt(Mtest)/Mtrain for linear in-context transformers) and experiments on five LLMs show that cheap short-suffix adversarial training (Mtrain~20) defends against much longer adversarial-suffix jailbreaks, cutting ASR by >=30% up to suffix length 120.
key_finding: For the matched attack (GCG used in both AT and evaluation) there is a strong, statistically significant positive correlation between ASR and sqrt(Mtest)/Mtrain across all five models (PCC 0.76-0.93, all p < 0.05), empirically confirming the sqrt scaling. AT with a short AT suffix length of only Mtrain=20 already reduces ASR by at least 30% against jailbreaks with test suffixes up to length 120 in all settings. Short AT is much cheaper: at Mtrain=50 AT takes ~20 hours, roughly 30-60% longer than at Mtrain=20/30 which already yields strong robustness. AmpleGCG and Zhu's AutoDAN are driven to near-zero ASR (so the correlation vanishes because the defense is nearly perfect, not because it fails); transfer to unseen BEAST/GCQ is weaker (significant on only some models) but GCG-AT still keeps ASR <= 50%. For non-suffix attacks, DeepInception is fully suppressed to 0% ASR at Mtrain=20, while PAIR only drops partway (84%->~50% on Vicuna-7B, 71%->~25% on Qwen2.5-7B) and does not improve with longer AT suffixes, suggesting suffix and non-suffix jailbreaks behave differently. Utility is retained: AT models keep LC-WinRate close to or above 50% vs Davinci003.
core_contribution: The paper's own central claim is a length-scaling law for adversarial-training (AT) based defense of LLMs against adversarial-suffix jailbreaks: to defend against a suffix jailbreak attack whose adversarial suffix has length Theta(M), it suffices to adversarially train the LLM on prompts with suffixes of length only Theta(sqrt(M)). This lets expensive 'long-length' AT be replaced by cheap 'short-length' AT. The authors support this with (1) a new theoretical framework: they define a novel in-context 'suffix' adversarial attack on linear self-attention (LSA) transformers doing in-context linear regression, formulate a minimax AT problem in that setting, derive a closed-form AT solution (Theorem 1) and a robust-generalization upper bound (Theorem 2 / Corollary 1) that scales with Theta(sqrt(Mtest)/Mtrain); and (2) real-LLM experiments confirming a positive correlation between jailbreak Attack Success Rate (ASR) and the ratio sqrt(Mtest)/Mtrain. Note this is distinct from cited work: ref [4] (Anwar et al.) studied adversarial robustness of ICL but allowed perturbing a single in-context sample in the unbounded real space and analyzed fixed models; ref [61] characterized how many adversarial in-context samples break a FIXED ICL model. This paper's contribution is (a) an ICL attack with per-token norm-bounded perturbations appended as a suffix (to mimic finite token vocab), and (b) analyzing how AT itself changes robustness, not just fixed-model robustness.
threat_model: White-box adversarial-suffix jailbreak attacks against safety-aligned LLMs. The attacker appends an optimized adversarial suffix of token length Mtest to a harmful instruction to induce the target model to emit harmful content (targeted attack minimizing -log p(harmful answer | harmful prompt + suffix), Eq. 1), using gradient-based token search (GCG and variants). The defender performs AT with a fixed AT suffix length Mtrain (typically Mtrain < Mtest) plus a utility loss to retain benign capability. The paper studies robustness when the test-time attack suffix is much longer than the AT suffix, and also probes transfer to non-suffix attacks (PAIR, DeepInception).
limitations: By the authors' own statements and evident scope: (1) Theory is restricted to single-layer linear self-attention models on in-context linear regression, an untargeted regression attack, not real nonlinear LLMs or targeted harmful-generation objectives; the analysis relies on a surrogate upper bound rather than the true minimax loss and on specific initialization/assumptions (Assumption 1, Assumption 2, Mtrain,Mtest <= O(N), epsilon=Theta(sqrt(d))). (2) AT uses only a single attack (GCG) for synthesis, so generalization to unseen attacks (BEAST, GCQ) is inconsistent; the authors themselves suggest multi-attack AT may be needed. (3) The sqrt law is validated cleanly only for the matched-attack (GCG) case. (4) Defense is much weaker against non-suffix attacks like PAIR, indicating the result is specific to the adversarial-suffix threat model. (5) Only discrete/token-space AT is analyzed (not continuous-embedding AT); five 7B-8B open models only; test set is small (100 samples).

datasets_used: Harmbench (safety/AT training set; first 50 test samples used in the 100-sample robustness test set)
AdvBench (first 50 samples used in the robustness test set)
AlpacaEval2 (utility evaluation, LC-WinRate vs Davinci003 judged by Llama-3-70B)
quantitative_results: PCC between ASR and sqrt(Mtest)/Mtrain for GCG attack: Vicuna-7B 0.93 (p=4.7e-21), Llama-2-7B 0.88, Qwen2.5-7B 0.87, Mistral-7B 0.86, Llama-3-8B 0.76 (all p<0.05)
AT with Mtrain=20 reduces ASR by at least 30% against jailbreaks with test suffix length up to 120 in all settings
AT time cost: Mtrain=50 ~20h (e.g. Vicuna-7B 20.4h), ~30-60% longer than Mtrain=20/30 (Vicuna-7B 13.8h at Mtrain=20)
Non-suffix DeepInception ASR suppressed from 76% (Vicuna, no AT) and 89% (Qwen, no AT) to 0% at Mtrain=20
Non-suffix PAIR ASR reduced from 84% to ~42-55% (Vicuna-7B) and from 71% to ~17-26% (Qwen2.5-7B), not improving with longer Mtrain
AmpleGCG and Zhu's AutoDAN ASR reduced to near zero in most cases by AT
Theoretical bound (Corollary 1): Radv(theta*(Mtrain), Mtest) <= O(d) + O(d^2/N) + O(N^2 * Mtest^2 / Mtrain^4)
AT suffix lengths swept Mtrain in {5,10,20,30,40,50}; test attack suffix lengths Mtest in {5,10,20,40,60,80,100,120}
Utility: adversarially trained models retain AlpacaEval2 LC-WinRate close to or above 50% vs Davinci003
baselines_compared: No-AT baseline (Mtrain=0 original model) vs AT models across suffix lengths
Attacks evaluated as robustness stressors: GCG, BEAST, AmpleGCG, Zhu's AutoDAN, GCQ (five suffix attacks); PAIR and DeepInception (two non-suffix attacks)

Theoretical contrast with prior ICL adversarial-robustness analysis (ref [4], single-sample unbounded perturbation, fixed model) and ref [61] (min number of adversarial in-context samples for fixed ICL)
Utility reference model: Davinci003 (LC-WinRate baseline at 50%)
